result:
The old tree in the back garden had been there longer than anyone could remember. Its roots had cracked the patio slabs decades ago, and now those cracks were filled with moss and tiny ferns, little green universes thriving in the shade of its monstrous trunk. I used to think it was just a tree—a big, gnarled oak that dropped acorns like hailstones in the autumn and groaned like a living thing when the wind picked up. But lately, I’ve started to think of it as a witness.

Think about it. That tree has seen everything this house has ever held. It watched the first owners plant roses that have long since gone wild and thorny. It saw the children of the 1950s swing from a rope tied to its highest branch, their laughter drifting up into the canopy like leaves. It endured the silent, dusty years when the house stood empty, the windows boarded, the grass knee-high. And now, it watches me—sitting here on the back step with a cup of coffee that’s gone cold, trying to untangle some knot in my own life that probably isn’t as complicated as I think it is.

There’s a strange comfort in that. To be seen by something that asks for nothing, judges nothing, and simply exists. It doesn’t care if I’m happy or sad, if I succeeded today or failed. It just grows. It stretches its roots deeper and its branches higher, one slow inch at a time, indifferent to the frantic little dramas unfolding beneath it.

Maybe that’s the lesson. Not that we should be indifferent, but that we should remember we’re part of something older and slower and quieter. We’re the leaves, not the trunk. We flutter and change color and fall, but the trunk stays. And next spring, new leaves will come.

I poured the cold coffee onto the soil at the base of the tree—a small offering, a thank you for the shade and the silence. The wind rustled the canopy, and for a moment, it almost sounded like a whisper. But it was probably just the wind.

Probably.
